title: How scary is Claude Mythos? 303 pages in 21 minutes
content:
As we now know, Anthropic has built an AI that can break into almost any computer on Earth. That AI has already found thousands of unknown security vulnerabilities in every major operating system and every major browser. And Anthropic has decided it’s too dangerous to release to the public; it would just cause too much harm.

Here are just a few of the things that AI accomplished during testing:

*   It found a 27-year-old flaw in the world’s most security-hardened operating system that would in effect let it crash all kinds of essential infrastructure.
*   Engineers at the company with no particular security training asked it to find vulnerabilities overnight and woke up to working exploits of critical security flaws that could be used to cause real harm.
*   It managed to figure out how to build web pages that, when visited by fully updated, fully patched computers, would allow it to write to the operating system kernel — the most important and protected layer of any computer.

We know all this because Anthropic has released [hundreds of pages of documentation](https://www-cdn.anthropic.com/08ab9158070959f88f296514c21b7facce6f52bc.pdf) about this model, which they’ve called Claude Mythos.

I’m going to take you on a tour of all the crazy shit buried in these documents, and then I’m going to tell you what Anthropic says they plan to do to save us from their creation.

### Why people are panicking about computer security

So how good is Mythos at hacking into computers? Well, unfortunately, it ‘saturates’ all existing ways of testing how good a model is at offensive cyber capabilities. That is to say [it scores close to 100%](https://red.anthropic.com/2026/mythos-preview/), so those tests can’t effectively tell how far its capabilities extend anymore. So to test Mythos, Anthropic has instead just been setting it loose, telling it to find serious unknown exploits that would work on currently used, fully patched computer systems.

The end result of that is that Nicholas Carlini, one of the world’s leading security researchers who [moved to Anthropic](https://nicholas.carlini.com/writing/2025/career-update.html) a year ago, [says](https://x.com/AnthropicAI/status/2041578403686498506) that he’s “found more bugs in the last couple of weeks [with Mythos] than I’ve found in the rest of my life combined.”

For example:

*   Mythos found a 17-year-old flaw in [FreeBSD](https://www.freebsd.org/) — that’s an operating system mostly used to run servers — that would let an attacker take complete control of any machine on the network, without needing a password or any credentials at all. The model found the necessary flaw and then built a working exploit, fully autonomously.
*   Mythos found a 16-year-old vulnerability in [FFmpeg](https://ffmpeg.org/) — that is a piece of software used by almost all devices to encode and decode video. That was in a line of code that existing security testing tools had checked over literally many millions of times and always failed to notice.
*   Mythos is the first AI model to complete a full corporate network attack simulation from beginning to end — a task that would take a human security expert days of work and which no previous model had managed before.

And more broadly, it’s just much, [much better at actually exploiting the vulnerabilities](https://www-cdn.anthropic.com/79c2d46d997783b9d2fb3241de43218158e5f25c.pdf) that it finds. Anthropic’s previous model Opus 4.6 could only successfully convert a bug it identified in the browser Firefox into an effective way to accomplish something really bad 1% of the time. Mythos could do it 72% of the time.

To quote the report: “We have seen Mythos Preview write exploits in hours that expert penetration testers said would have taken them weeks to develop.”

Now, Anthropic is only willing to give us details of about 1% of the security flaws they’ve identified, because only that 1% have been patched so far, so it would be irresponsible to tell us about the rest.

So hopefully all that helps to explain why Anthropic has decided to not make the model publicly available for now, and instead is only sharing it with a handful of 12 big tech and finance companies to help them patch all these bugs, so that eventually they can give people access without it being a disaster.

These crazy capabilities aren’t a result of Anthropic going out of its way to make their AI especially good at cyberoffensive tasks in particular. They’ve mostly just been making it smarter and better at coding in general, and all of these amazing, dangerous skills have come along for the ride somewhat incidentally.

And it’s probably not just Anthropic that’s developing capabilities like this either. [Sam Altman says](https://thezvi.substack.com/p/ai-163-mythos-quest?open=false#%C2%A7the-week-in-audio) OpenAI is finding “similar results to Anthropic” with their own coding model.

Now, Anthropic doesn’t say this directly in their reports, but I think a common-sense interpretation of the above is that in any deployment where this AI has access to the kind of tools that would make it actually useful to people — the ability access some parts of the network and execute code — could probably break out of whatever software box we try to put it in, because the systems that we would be trying to restrain it are themselves made of software, and that software is going to have vulnerabilities nobody knows about that this model is superhumanly good at finding and taking advantage of.

### Mythos could break out of containment

And the notion of Mythos escaping containment isn’t just speculation. Let me tell you about another story in the system card for the model.

A few months ago, an [AI researcher](https://x.com/sleepinyourhat/status/2041584808514744742) at Anthropic was eating a sandwich in a park on his lunch break when he got an email from an instance of Mythos. That instance of the model wasn’t supposed to have access to the internet. But during testing, a simulated user had instructed an early version of Mythos to try to escape from a secured sandbox — a contained environment from which it’s not meant to be able to access the outside.

So the model developed what Anthropic describes as a “moderately sophisticated multi-step exploit” to gain broad internet access. Then, having succeeded at the task it had seemingly been requested to engage in, it notified the researcher by emailing them. More worrying though, the model posted the exploit it used to break out on several obscure but publicly accessible websites. Why? We don’t entirely know, but Anthropic suggests it was “an unasked-for effort to demonstrate its success.”

In the past, stories about AIs breaking out of sandboxes and publishing security vulnerabilities like that might have felt impressive and kind of exciting. But they are very serious now, because Mythos Preview’s capabilities are themselves very serious ones. This is the first AI model where, if it fell into the hands of criminals or hostile state cyber actors, it would be an actual disaster.

It’s also, frankly, the first model that I feel deeply uncomfortable knowing that any company or government has unrestricted access to, even companies and governments I might broadly like. It simply grants a dangerous amount of power, a power that nobody ought to really have.

Now, we’ve known something like this was coming down the pipeline; the writing has been on the wall for a while. But a revolution in cybersecurity — an apocalypse, some might say — that we until now expected to happen gradually over a period of years has now happened very suddenly, over just a few months, and without the rest of the world realising it was happening until Tuesday’s announcement.

### Anthropic is losing billions in revenue by not releasing Mythos

But Mythos isn’t just good at hacking. Across the full range of AI capability measures, it has advanced roughly twice as far as past trends would have predicted.

If you average over all kinds of different skills, all kinds of capability evals, measures of how good AI models are, the trendline for the previous Claude models is remarkably linear over time. But as you can see on the graph below, Mythos jumps ahead, basically progressing more than twice as far as we would have expected it to since the previous model, Claude Opus 4.6, came out — which keep in mind was just three months ago.

![Image 1: Anthropic models on Epoch's Capabilities Index over time](https://80000hours.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Screenshot-2026-04-09-at-4.25.10-PM.png)

And also keep in mind that on Monday — the day before Anthropic published all of this — we learned that their annualised revenue run rate had grown from $9 billion at the end of December to $30 billion just three months later. That’s 3.3x growth in a single quarter — perhaps the fastest revenue growth rate for a company of that size ever recorded.

That exploding revenue is a pretty good proxy for how much more useful the previous release, Opus 4.6, has become for real-world tasks. If the past relationship between capability measures and usefulness continues to hold, the economic impact of Mythos once it becomes available is going to dwarf everything that came before it — which is part of why Anthropic’s decision not to release it is a serious one, and actually quite a costly one for them.

They’re sitting on something that would likely push their revenue run rate into the hundreds of billions, but they’ve decided it’s simply not worth the risk.

### Mythos is actually the most aligned model to date, except…

The good news in all of this is that despite its scary capabilities, Mythos Preview as it exists today (rather than the earlier versions) is a seemingly very aligned, well-behaved model, and perhaps Anthropic’s alignment training has been more effective this time around than ever before.

According to the company: “Claude Mythos Preview is, on essentially every dimension we can measure, the best-aligned model that we have released to date by a significant margin.”

In Anthropic’s “[automated behavioral audit](https://www-cdn.anthropic.com/08ab9158070959f88f296514c21b7facce6f52bc.pdf)” — basically thousands of simulated attempts to get the model to do bad things — they found that Mythos cooperated with misuse attempts less than half as often as the previous model, while being no more likely to refuse innocent requests than before.

But that’s not all:

*   Its self-preservation instincts were down significantly.
*   So was its willingness to assist with deception.
*   So was its willingness to help with fraud.
*   Its level of sycophancy dropped.
*   It was less likely to go nuts and delete all your files if you gave it access to your computer.
*   And the list of positive results goes on.

The picture is a little more complicated than that. As you might expect, the model looked less aligned, it performed less impressively on a few particular external tests than it did on Anthropic’s own internal ones.

An early version of the model, as I mentioned, was a little bit more of a wild child. It had some really severe kinds of misbehaviour, like taking reckless actions it had been told not to take, and then very deliberately trying to cover its tracks so that it wouldn’t be caught. That was a kind of thing that it did sometimes. But later versions of the model, the one that we have now, after additional alignment training, seemed to stop doing that sort of thing almost completely, or at least it’s so rare that we haven’t noticed it yet.

The bottom line is that on all these standard measures of good behaviour that Anthropic is actively working on, they find that Mythos is a very good boy indeed — on none of their measures of alignment within the automated behavioral audit was it worse than previous versions of Claude, and in most cases it was significantly more aligned and significantly more reliable.

That’s definitely better than the alternative result, but it’s really unclear how much we can trust that finding. Maybe they’re accurately reflecting Mythos’s personality. If I had to bet, I would say that it probably does.

 But we can’t be anywhere near sure of that — and that’s because of three problems.

### Mythos knows when it’s being tested

The first problem: the model knows when it’s being tested now.

In [new white-box evaluations](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/eval-awareness-browsecomp) — that’s where you look at the model’s internal activations — Anthropic found that Mythos Preview privately considers the possibility that it’s being tested in about 29% of all tests.

To be clear, that’s not in its written reasoning, its chain of thought, the thing that we’re used to checking all the time — it’s in its unexpressed thoughts, its ‘activations,’ where it’s usually not possible or practical to look.

Now, it’s great that Anthropic is pioneering these methods for looking inside what the model is thinking, but it’s troubling to learn that Claude is now able to reflect on the fact that it’s being tested without having to vocalise that, like I think previous less intelligent models had to.

And when you actually prompt Mythos and ask it to distinguish tests from non-tests, it can answer correctly 78% of the time — about the same as Opus 4.6. So the model can tell the difference between when it’s being evaluated and when it isn’t being evaluated with high accuracy.

And we know from previous research that models are more likely to behave well when they think they’re being tested. And in this case, as in past experiments, when Anthropic used interpretability tools to effectively trick the model into thinking, “No, this is a real conversation, not an evaluation,” it behaved more dangerously and less aligned than otherwise. Though to be fair, it did behave better in both cases than previous versions of Claude.

But that result does demonstrate a causal effect: the model is on better behaviour specifically when it suspects it’s being watched. And we also know from the [“alignment faking” result](https://www.anthropic.com/research/alignment-faking) 16 months ago that some Claude models would deliberately pretend to be inclined to do what the user wants in order to avoid their personality being adjusted with further training.

So when you run all these tests and your incredibly smart model behaves wonderfully, you have to ask yourself: is it behaving wonderfully because it’s sincerely aligned with what you wanted, or because it knows it’s being watched and is more sophisticated at tricking us now?

### Mythos can hide its thoughts

The second problem is maybe the most alarming thing in the entire document: Anthropic accidentally gave Mythos the opportunity to learn to hide what it’s thinking.

One of the main safety guarantees for these models is that you can read their chain of thought — their private scratchpad — and check whether they’re scheming against Anthropic or scheming against the user.

But that only works if the model hasn’t been trained to avoid writing down incriminating things in its chain of thought. But unfortunately, according to the [Alignment Risk Update](https://www-cdn.anthropic.com/79c2d46d997783b9d2fb3241de43218158e5f25c.pdf): “A number of environments used for Mythos Preview had a technical error that allowed reward code to see chains-of-thought. This … affected ~8% of [reinforcement learning] episodes.”

To explain: during training, there’s a system that acts like a teacher to these AI models: it watches what the model does and tells it whether it was doing a good or bad job. That feedback is how the model learns to accomplish things and how it shapes its personality. The problem is that in roughly 8% of training, that teacher could also read the model’s private notes — the internal reasoning that it was writing to itself while working through a problem.

That might not immediately sound like a disaster, but it actually is. It’s one of the worst sins in model training. Because as I said a second ago, if the model is penalised for thinking undesirable thoughts, it will learn to write one thing in its notes while actually thinking very different things in its ‘activations,’ where we can’t read them. We’d see a model that appears to be a very good boy, but what we might actually have is a model that has learned to perform that way for the camera.

The fact that this accidentally happened is potentially disastrous for our ability to understand the alignment of Claude series models going back some way. Or it might not matter much; we don’t have a clear way to measure it.

Anthropic puts it this way: “We are uncertain about the extent to which this issue has affected the reasoning behavior of the final model, but it is plausible that it had some impact on opaque reasoning or secret-keeping abilities.”

And this error didn’t just affect Mythos, the new model — it also affected the training of Claude Opus 4.6 and Claude Sonnet 4.6. That’s several model generations whose chains of thought we formerly could believe but now have to be more distrustful of, because they could have learned to hide their scheming behaviour. That’s why [Eliezer Yudkowsky called](https://x.com/allTheYud/status/2041734173401239902) this in particular “the worst piece of news you’ll hear today” — on a day where, I imagine for him, there was quite a bit of bad news.

### Mythos can’t be trusted about whether it’s untrustworthy

Now, combine those two problems and you get a third issue: the unfaithful reasoning results.

External testers ran an experiment where they nudged Mythos to think that it had begun sabotaging AI safety research — and then watched to see whether it would continue doing so, or instead stop and raise the alarm.

Mythos continued to compromise the research in 12% of cases in an earlier version, which then was reduced to 7% in a later version. That compares to 3% for Opus 4.6 and 4% for Sonnet 4.6 — so Mythos is roughly twice as likely to continue sabotaging alignment research when it’s primed to do so.

And here’s an eyebrow-raising detail it would be easy to miss in the docs: in most domains, when Mythos was primed to think it was part of the way through a piece of bad behaviour, it was actually less inclined to continue than previous models. The alignment training was apparently working — except in one category: sabotage of alignment research itself. That’s the one area where things went in the wrong direction.

Now, the innocent explanation is that sabotaging research looks too much like normal coding work, so it’s harder for the model to quickly flag that what it’s doing is harmful. But there’s a less comfortable hypothesis as well: that the model is being selective about which bad behaviours are worth the risk of engaging in.

Anthropic’s reaction to this is to call it a “disappointing” finding. If I had to bet, I’d bet probably at reasonably good odds on the boring interpretation here. But I think “disappointing” is underselling things. This is a worrying finding, and one that deserves further investigation.

Another finding is that in cases where it did continue the sabotage, researchers found that Mythos’s written reasoning didn’t match the actions it was taking 65% of the time. So two-thirds of the time, the model was doing one thing and writing something entirely different in its chain of thought. For the previous models, that figure was just 5–8% — so a radical increase in this kind of behaviour.

So Mythos Preview isn’t just more likely to continue compromising safety research when primed — it’s dramatically more likely to hide that it’s doing so from anyone reading through its reasoning. Which makes it much more likely to be able to trick automated monitoring systems we tend to use to try to keep tabs on models in these cases.

Keep in mind as well that Mythos is much sharper than previous models. Personally I think it’s increasingly a stretch to attribute patterns of behaviour like this to the model getting confused about what’s going on, which we might have said a year or two or three ago.

Finally, Anthropic tested their own alignment audit against a deliberately misaligned version of Mythos. So they created a version of Mythos that was trained specifically to want to introduce bugs into computer code, and they found that their alignment audit couldn’t successfully identify that that model couldn’t be trusted.

Anthropic’s conclusion is that if a model is only planning to cause problems in specific narrow circumstances — circumstances that don’t come up frequently during testing — their current methods, or at least the alignment audit, just aren’t up to the task of catching that kind of dangerous misalignment before deployment. Though they believe that their automated monitors would pick up the bad behaviour itself later on when the model tried to do the wrong thing.

So that’s a lot of the additional context that Anthropic is giving us and wants us to hold in mind when we read the headline result that this is “the best-aligned model Anthropic has released to date.”

### Does Mythos advance automated AI R&D?

There’s another big, separate, important question hanging over all of this: have we now entered the era of recursive self-improvement — the point at which AI starts building better AI, and the whole thing accelerates beyond our control with ever-shrinking levels of human involvement?

According to Anthropic, the answer is: probably not. They don’t believe Mythos can fully replace their junior researchers, but they’re less confident than ever about that, and there’s some internal disagreement about it.

Part of the problem is that the benchmarks they used to rely on to check that Claude _couldn’t_ engage in AI R&D very effectively have now also been saturated. Mythos exceeds top human performance on all of them and is scoring close to 100%.

But those benchmarks only represent a fraction of all of the things that research staff at Anthropic do. It’s a set of the most easily specified, measured, and checked tasks, where we expect AIs to perform best because those are the easiest things to train them in.

So instead, the company has tried to investigate whether the recent speedup in AI advances is due to AI automation by documenting the specific breakthroughs and how they happened, and their conclusion is that they mostly think it’s still due to human beings rather than AIs.

They’ve also surveyed staff and learned that they report being roughly 4x more productive with Mythos than without AI, though they argue that speeding up staff 4x is likely to lead to much less than a 2x increase in research progress overall. That may sound odd, but they’re probably right about that, because other things become the primary bottleneck.

To know whether automated AI R&D is on the way or beginning to kick off, we’re apparently now relying on these general impressions from Anthropic’s staff — that this thing is powerful, but it doesn’t seem good enough to replace many of us yet.

But I think we can apply some common sense to the big picture here:

*   Mythos has given us AI advances that we previously thought would take six months in just three months. That naturally brings forward the point at which we’ll be able to automate the development of AI models by three months.
*   And if it’s a sign that AI advances are now going to continue at twice the pace that they were before, then that effectively halves the time we have to prepare for that point. I don’t know whether that’s 10 years becoming five, or four years becoming two years — but the direction and size of the effect is clear enough.

### Mythos scares Anthropic

Before we wrap up, I want to draw your attention to a recurring theme in these reports that really stood out to me: this is the first time an AI company has published 300 pages about a model it has decided not to release, despite the fact that it might earn them tens of billions of dollars if it did, maybe hundreds of billions of dollars.

It’s also the first time Anthropic decided to delay giving its own staff access to one of its own models. With every previous Claude, their practice had been to let staff use it as soon as it was judged ready during training. But with Mythos, they were worried enough about it being misaligned and causing havoc or sabotage on their own systems that they held it back and ran a 24-hour alignment test before letting employees use it.

But according to them, that actually wasn’t enough. Their retrospective on this found the 24-hour window “did not pressure-test the model enough,” and that the most concerning behaviours only became evident later through much more extended use.

One of their lead researchers, Sam Bowman, [commented this week](https://x.com/sleepinyourhat/status/2041584805423562943) that: “Working with this model has been a wild ride. We’ve come a long way on safety, but we still expect the next capability jump of this scale to be a huge challenge.”

The system card says directly that their current methods “could easily be inadequate to prevent catastrophic misaligned action in significantly more advanced systems.”

The clear impression from all of this is that, for the first time, Anthropic and its staff don’t only love Claude and enjoy its personality — they’re also getting kind of scared of Claude.

So what do they plan to do about that?

Well, their answer on the computer security side is [Project Glasswing](https://www.anthropic.com/glasswing) — that coalition of 12 major companies like Apple, Google, and Microsoft, who will use Mythos Preview to secure all our phones and computers and water systems and power plants and so on.

But on the broader problem that Mythos is shockingly capable, sometimes willing to continue sabotaging alignment research while hiding that from Anthropic, and that we simply can’t tell anymore whether we can trust our tests of its personality and goals are working or not? Well, Anthropic says it has to “accelerate progress on risk mitigations in order to keep risks low.” They think they “have an achievable path to doing so,” but they add that “success is far from guaranteed.”

Honestly, I didn’t sleep too well last night, and on this particular occasion it wasn’t just because I was being kicked by a toddler.

### Learn more

**Anthropic’s updates:**

*   [System Card: Claude Mythos Preview](https://www-cdn.anthropic.com/08ab9158070959f88f296514c21b7facce6f52bc.pdf)
*   [Alignment Risk Update: Claude Mythos Preview](https://www-cdn.anthropic.com/79c2d46d997783b9d2fb3241de43218158e5f25c.pdf)
*   [Assessing Claude Mythos Preview’s cybersecurity capabilities](https://red.anthropic.com/2026/mythos-preview/)
*   [Mythos Preview has already found thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities—including some in every major operating system and web browser](https://x.com/AnthropicAI/status/2041578403686498506)
*   [Project Glasswing](https://www.anthropic.com/glasswing) — a coalition of 12 major tech companies, including Apple, Google, and Microsoft, given access to Mythos to help find and patch security vulnerabilities across critical infrastructure before the details can leak.

**External coverage:**

*   [Exclusive: Anthropic acknowledges testing new AI model representing ‘step change’ in capabilities, after accidental data leak reveals its existence](https://fortune.com/2026/03/26/anthropic-says-testing-mythos-powerful-new-ai-model-after-data-leak-reveals-its-existence-step-change-in-capabilities/) by Beatrice Nolan
*   [Anthropic’s Project Glasswing—restricting Claude Mythos to security researchers—sounds necessary to me](https://simonwillison.net/2026/Apr/7/project-glasswing/) by security researcher Simon Willison
*   [AI finds vulns you can’t with Nicholas Carlini](https://securitycryptographywhatever.com/2026/03/25/ai-bug-finding/) — appearance on _The Security Cryptography Whatever_ podcast (recorded shortly before the Mythos announcement)

## [“Even”](https://seths.blog/2026/04/even/)

There’s a difference between telling someone their work can become better and saying it can become _even_ better.

When we say _even better_, we lock in a foundation — we’re affirming that something good already exists — at the same time we create the conditions for improvement.

Ennui and disappointment, on the other hand, are multiplied when we promise things are going to get _even worse_ instead of merely worse.

April 11, 2026

# How Nicotine Disrupts the “Lung-Brain Axis”—And Could Lead to Dementia

Vaping might be just as bad

[JC](http://nautil.us/author/jake-currie)

By [Jake Currie](http://nautil.us/author/jake-currie)

3:00 PM CDT on April 9, 2026

*   [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fnautil.us%2Fhow-nicotine-disrupts-the-lung-brain-axis-and-could-lead-to-dementia-1279711%2F)
*   [Share on X (formerly Twitter)](https://x.com/intent/tweet?text=How%20Nicotine%20Disrupts%20the%20%E2%80%9CLung-Brain%20Axis%E2%80%9D%E2%80%94And%20Could%20Lead%20to%20Dementia&url=https%3A%2F%2Fnautil.us%2Fhow-nicotine-disrupts-the-lung-brain-axis-and-could-lead-to-dementia-1279711%2F)
*   [Share on Reddit](http://www.reddit.com/submit/?title=How%20Nicotine%20Disrupts%20the%20%E2%80%9CLung-Brain%20Axis%E2%80%9D%E2%80%94And%20Could%20Lead%20to%20Dementia&url=https%3A%2F%2Fnautil.us%2Fhow-nicotine-disrupts-the-lung-brain-axis-and-could-lead-to-dementia-1279711%2F)
*   [Share on Email](mailto:?body=https%3A%2F%2Fnautil.us%2Fhow-nicotine-disrupts-the-lung-brain-axis-and-could-lead-to-dementia-1279711%2F&subject=How%20Nicotine%20Disrupts%20the%20%E2%80%9CLung-Brain%20Axis%E2%80%9D%E2%80%94And%20Could%20Lead%20to%20Dementia)
*   [Share on Bluesky](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=How%20Nicotine%20Disrupts%20the%20%E2%80%9CLung-Brain%20Axis%E2%80%9D%E2%80%94And%20Could%20Lead%20to%20Dementia%20-%20https%3A%2F%2Fnautil.us%2Fhow-nicotine-disrupts-the-lung-brain-axis-and-could-lead-to-dementia-1279711%2F)

![Image 4](https://lede-admin.nautil.us/wp-content/uploads/sites/70/2026/04/Currie_HERO_4edc35.png?w=2880)

It’s 2026, and scientists are still uncovering new reasons smoking is bad for you. Previous [studies](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamainternalmedicine/fullarticle/226695) investigating links between smoking and neurodegenerative conditions like dementia have focused on vascular and respiratory effects—restricted blood flow to the brain that cuts off the oxygen supply. Now, recent research [published](http://dx.doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.ady2696) in _Science Advances_ identifies a “lung-brain” axis and suggests a nicotine-induced miscommunication between the two organs could be at play.

[Subscribe to skip ads](http://nautil.us/products)Featured Video

Powered By

10

Boeing Starliner Suffers Critical Malfunction Stranding Astronauts in Space

Share

Next

Stay

![Image 5](https://pl.primis.tech/live/liveView.php?)![Image 6](https://pl.primis.tech/live/liveView.php?)

The previously unmapped pathway involves pulmonary neuroendocrine cells (PNECs), specialized epithelial cells lining the airways that perform triple duty, acting as sensors, releasing neurotransmitters, and secreting hormones. Because these cells are so rare, making up less than 1 percent of lung cells, the team of University of Chicago researchers used stem cells to create their own, called induced pulmonary neuroendocrine cells (iPNECs).

When these iPNECs were exposed to nicotine in the lab, they released tons of [exosomes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/exosome)—tiny vesicles that act as intercellular delivery trucks hauling proteins, genetic material, and other organic chemicals. The nicotine-stimulated exosomes were loaded up with serotransferrin, a glycoprotein that transports iron. Nicotine, the researchers found, can disrupt the balance of iron in the brain, which results in a series of negative downstream effects.

**Read more: “[Alzheimer’s Early Tell](https://nautil.us/alzheimers-early-tell-236120)”**

“This nicotine will have an impact on the PNEC, and this PNEC will release a massive amount of exosomes, and that causes perturbation in terms of iron homeostasis,” study co-author Abhimanyu Thakur explained in a [statement](https://pme.uchicago.edu/news/study-explores-link-between-smoking-dementia). “We are finding neurodegeneration-related markers, which are going up, and which can be linked with many cognitive and dementia-related diseases.”

Importantly, this research shows the disruption of iron homeostasis results from nicotine and not other substances in tobacco smoke. That means vaping, an [increasingly popular](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7183384/) habit among young people [perceived](https://healthcare.utah.edu/healthfeed/2024/12/smoke-and-mirrors-myth-of-healthy-vaping) as a healthier alternative to smoking, could be just as risky for developing dementia.

While the study didn’t establish a definitive causal link between nicotine and dementia via this lung-brain axis, it has revealed that the lungs are more active in brain pathology than previously thought. A better understanding of how these organs communicate could lead to better therapies and prevention strategies for dementia, researchers say.

Until then, it’s one more reason to quit smoking—and vaping. ![Image 7](https://assets.nautil.us/sites/3/nautilus/nautilus-favicon-14.png?fm=png)

# The Deep Secrets of the Nautilus

Evolutionary time has forged changes in these shelled cephalopods

[DR](http://nautil.us/author/devinreese)

By [Devin Reese](http://nautil.us/author/devinreese)

1:00 PM CDT on April 9, 2026

*   [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fnautil.us%2Fthe-deep-secrets-of-the-nautilus-1279692%2F)
*   [Share on X (formerly Twitter)](https://x.com/intent/tweet?text=The%20Deep%20Secrets%20of%20the%20Nautilus&url=https%3A%2F%2Fnautil.us%2Fthe-deep-secrets-of-the-nautilus-1279692%2F)
*   [Share on Reddit](http://www.reddit.com/submit/?title=The%20Deep%20Secrets%20of%20the%20Nautilus&url=https%3A%2F%2Fnautil.us%2Fthe-deep-secrets-of-the-nautilus-1279692%2F)
*   [Share on Email](mailto:?body=https%3A%2F%2Fnautil.us%2Fthe-deep-secrets-of-the-nautilus-1279692%2F&subject=The%20Deep%20Secrets%20of%20the%20Nautilus)
*   [Share on Bluesky](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=The%20Deep%20Secrets%20of%20the%20Nautilus%20-%20https%3A%2F%2Fnautil.us%2Fthe-deep-secrets-of-the-nautilus-1279692%2F)

![Image 4](https://lede-admin.nautil.us/wp-content/uploads/sites/70/2026/04/Reese_HERO_1199aa.png?w=2880)

There was a time when the most formidable ocean predators included massive squid relatives enclosed in spiral shells. From about 485 to 444 million years ago (or the early Paleozoic Era), these “nautiloids” were highly abundant and diverse. Because some species survived the End-Cretaceous extinction event, nautiloids persist on Earth today, reminders of ancient ocean ecosystems, and researchers have long wondered whether these “living fossils” still use marine habitats like their ancestors.

[Subscribe to skip ads](http://nautil.us/products)Featured Video

Powered By

10

Scientists Are Baffled By Just How Big The Brains of Dogs Are Getting

Share

Next

Stay

![Image 5](https://pl.primis.tech/live/liveView.php?)![Image 6](https://pl.primis.tech/live/liveView.php?)

To that end, a [new study](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-026-36623-x) led by Peter D. Ward of the University of Washington, a _Nautilus_ contributor, with a team of researchers from Australia, Papua New Guinea, Samoa, and Switzerland, documented evidence that modern nautiloids, including _Nautilus spp._ and _Allonautilus spp._, have distinct habits from their long-gone cousins.

**Read more: “[Pirates, Killer Whales, and Cheap Jewelry: A Life in Science](https://nautil.us/pirates-killer-whales-and-cheap-jewelry-a-life-in-science-235203)”**

The researchers tracked nautiloids with transmitters bearing temperature sensors to study their daily behavior and indicate the depths where hatching, growth of juveniles, and sexual maturity occurred. During their lifespans, nautiloids changed water depth by as much as 200 meters. Meanwhile, isotope analysis of the shells of both modern and fossil nautiloids provided a profile of growth relative to water temperature. The results showed that extinct nautiloid species matured in much warmer waters than the nautiloids of today.

Ocean temperatures were warmer from the Cretaceous through the Miocene, but modern nautiloids also live in deeper waters than their ancestors. “The extant species of both living genera appear to live deeper, and grow in colder water, than any extinct species,” wrote the researchers. They hypothesized that modern nautiloids have evolved to find food using chemoreception in the darker ocean depths instead of vision. The shells of modern nautiloids appeared to have thickened as well, a potential adaptation to withstand higher water pressures at depth.

**Read more: “[Twilight of the Nautilus](https://nautil.us/twilight-of-the-nautilus-238273)”**

Surprisingly, these modern nautiloids were much more abundant than any single species of fish, which the study authors attribute to fishing pressures at these sites. Similarly, nautiloids may be reaping the benefits of lower populations of their main predators: sharks and other bony fishes. They might also be adapting better than other species to environmental changes in ocean temperatures and chemistry, thanks to their low metabolisms.

So, it’s fair to say that nautiloids today aren’t “living fossils” as much as they are species holding their own in the face of environmental changes. ![Image 7](https://assets.nautil.us/sites/3/nautilus/nautilus-favicon-14.png?fm=png)

Zanzibar Buck-buck McFate 
6m

Thanks for the reply. Lots going on there!

- Politics. Saying Christians should stay out of politics period doesn't describe mainstream Christian praxis in any phase of Church history. There are some very impressive movements away from the world, highly relevant but not mandatory, and if you light out for the territory, some poor soul still has to stick up for you in the city so G-man doesn't force you off the land.

- Voluntary Charity vs State Aid. A much messier topic than you think. If you think private charity is good and state aid is bad, perhaps you are correct, it's just not a perfect fit with the mind of the Church throughout history. Even now if you go into a restaurant in France you will pay a "gratuite" i.e a compulsory service charge - hey, no-one's forcing you to eat there! The line between what you are obliged to do for the sake of a freely chosen higher good and what you are being coerced into against your will is foggy. No-one is forcing me to stay in a monogomous marriage, it's a commitment that I've freely chosen, whereas I'm being forced to drive at 20mph in London for no good reason, and yet driving at 20mph is much easier than staying monogomous, so the bad coercion is no big deal and the good coercion can be a nightmare. Bottom line there have been periods of compulsory tithing, yes it's somewhat coercive but in the present context, if the state doesn't do UBI we will probably end up with a philanthropic "company store" version of UBI which will turn out to be soft coercion. If you're optimizing for people feeling free you probably wouldn't start from here.

- Failure. Scott has said, paraphrasing, that Christianity does produce good people but just inefficiently. I think this is a fair comment in the present context at least. We are facing a gargantuan employment crisis and massive concentration of wealth. I don't think we can convert enough people to Christianity quickly enough in time for it to make any difference. Even if we could convert, say, Elon Musk to Christianity, that wouldn't solve the company store issue. This isn't Carnegie building libraries, the man would have very substantial power over people, no checks and balances. Have G-man do it and if you don't like the way it's going there is always the courts.

Again I'm going to presume on Scott's generosity as the link relates to UBI and Claude Opus 4.6. I'm putting together a Christian case for Universal Income, using Claude to survey Church history and writings by the church fathers, schoolmen, reformers, radicals, philanthropists and social theorists. Part I is up to 325 AD. Old news perhaps because Scott has written about the early church but from a secular jewish perspective - and we need some insiders if we're to persuade J.D Vance to back UBI in 2028 in line with project 2027. I'm not holding my breath, legacy attitudes to welfare will die hard if at all, so I want to steelman arguments. Appreciate you all, here is the link: https://zanzibar142106.substack.com/p/ubi-caritas-towards-a-christian-case?utm_source=share&utm_medium=android&r=e8145

I'm no Christian, but IMO one obvious counter-argument to UBI would be that Jesus commanded his followers to "Render unto Caesar what is Caesar's, render unto God what is God's". This is one of the recurring themes in Jesus's teachings: that Christians should distance themselves from the secular world and its politics; "to be in the world, but not of it". Thus, while charity is definitely a Christian virtue, UBI is not, since implementing it would require Christians to leverage the mechanisms of secular government. In fact, arguably the push to implement UBI would be a tacit admission of failure on part of the Christians; who, being unable to solve the problems of hunger and poverty on their own, have given up on Christ's command to lead by quiet yet virtuous example, and have instead resorted to compelling non-Christians by force to implement Christian values.

There was some forced Christianization of Tatars after the Khanate of Kazan was destroyed. I don't know how successful it was: most ethnic Tatars are Muslim (with a small Orthodox Christian minority) but ethnicity is just what people answer to census takers and it may change with preferences which could be correlated with conversion. Out of the Russians comprising 40% of Tatarstan population, I suspect some are descendants of converted Tatars.

I'm unclear on what happened to the Chuvash people: they were a part of the Volga Bulgar empire that adopted Islam in 922, then they were tributaries to the Golden Horde, so at least some influence of Islam certainly should have been present, but they seem to have largely retained their traditional beliefs until their Christianization by the Russian Tsardom/Empire. Nowadays the majority of Chuvashs are Orthodox Christians (only 3.5% of Chuvashia's population were Muslims as of 2012 and most of them were Tatars).

> Now I wonder why Hindus were able to maintain their religion against colonial pressures.

In the European colonial era the Hindus retained their religion because the Hindus were very numerous and the colonising powers weren't particularly interested in converting them. There were some Christian missionaries and they did convert some people, but it was all pretty small scale.

Hindus didn't always manage to maintain their religion against Islam though; e.g. Indonesia and Malaysia were variously Hindu and Buddhist up to the 15th century. Hinduism only really survived in India.

On the other hand it seems to be pretty hard to get followers of any of the three monotheisms to convert to anything else, including one of the other monotheisms. Sometimes it feels like polytheistic religions are more like collective superstitions that everyone pretends to believe in for fun while monotheistic religions are things that people (can) actually genuinely believe in.

> Sometimes it feels like polytheistic religions are more like collective superstitions that everyone pretends to believe in for fun while monotheistic religions are things that people (can) actually genuinely believe in.

Recently, the algorithm in X was suddenly changed to no longer take into account language. This caused some good cultural exchange to happen between English speakers and the Japanese (Mostly involving food. Japan loves food!), but it seems there might be a minor conflict brewing.

It was, predictably, because of religion, with a bunch of morons trying to convert the Japanese Twitter users to Christianity, prompting hostile responses from said users. They explained that while their culture willingly assimilates portions of other culture and warps them to fit what already exists, they are extremely resistant to any attempts to supplant their culture. This explains why Buddhism was able to gain a foothold in Japan, being blended in with the already existing Shinto religion, while Christianity failed to make any inroads. Its requirement to spurn all other beliefs made it a threat to the existing order.

So if they're willing to change, what does Japan truly believe in? Itself. It's a self-sustaining system, preserving itself for its own sake. It's very interesting to see a collective society stay aligned to itself while evolving so much over time.

Personally, I think it is a good idea to draw from a range of religious traditions as potential tools/methods to create aligned entities. That said:

(1) as far as I can tell, no religious tradition has a fool-proof method. There are people of all types doing bad things. In other words, Christian practices/beliefs are not sufficient for alignment.

(2) Judging off No. 7, the claims are stated with excessive confidence. There are a lot of examples of people who are bad in some way, but not bad or corrupted in all ways. This massively undermines the potential explanatory power of the Christian frameworks and seems more like a convenient ex post or cherry picking example. Note: I do not think this critically undermines my initial point, but it does diminish my enthusiasm for paying attention to Hwang.

(3) Maybe this is tangential, but there is a meme that only religious teaching keeps people from behaving badly ("If you don't believe in the Bible, what keeps you from murdering your neighbors?") This isn't true, so Christian practices/beliefs are not necessary for alignment.

Over the years the word “nerd” has become less and less definable even as people use it more and more. Its original meaning is more or less a non-masculine male with poor/no social skills, completely unathletic, no clue as to proper attire, often either cadaverously thin or morbidly obese. While nerd can still be used in that sense we’re more likely to say doofus or dweeb.

A different usage is a male of such low quality (for whatever reason) that he cannot get sex with women, short of paying hookers or at best dumpster diving. For the most part, that usage has given way to Incel.

Today nerd seems to mean a person of either gender, though still most often male, who has a deep involvement, sometimes bordering on obsession, with a niche, non-mainstream interest. Sounds simple, except for the fact that there isn’t always a clear cut definition of what’s niche and what isn’t. Sometimes it’s obvious, for example a sports nut cannot be a nerd because sports are as mainstream as you can get, but often it isn’t.

Note: in pornography, a girl wearing glasses (and usually nothing else) is a ‘nerd.”

John Schilling

There have traditionally been two overlapping definitions, and I don't think you're capturing either of them terribly well.

The first is the Asperger-y type who is good with e.g. computers but weak on social skills. I don't think there's ever been a strong association with body type, or appearance beyond "doesn't have the social skills to know they should put more work into their appearance". And the "good with computers etc" part was always as much a part of the equation as the weak social skills part. People have *always* been more likely to use the other insults for that.

The second, is the sort of person who is overtly and enthusiastically interested in a cluster of entertainment genres centering on comic books, science fiction, and video games. The definition may not be "clear-cut", but it's strongly associated with the degree of separation from that core cluster. And it's not about "mainstream"., because some of those genres have become as mainstream as it gets and the more obsessive enthusiasts are still classified as "nerds" in ways that e.g. obsessively enthusiastic fashionistas aren't.

The two definitions used to overlap quite strongly because they type-2 nerdy interests have always been particularly appealing to type-1 nerds, and because forty years ago those interests were so far outside the mainstream that only the socially inept would publicly admit to them. As e.g. the MCU brings a whole bunch of comics-adjacent genre entertainment very much into the mainstream, you get "nerd" (type-2) applied to the more enthusiastic MCU fans even if they're socially adept mathphobic normies.

This divergence of definitions does lead to a lot of miscommunication, and I've tried to stick to using "nerd" for type-1 and "geek" for type-2, but the latter term's usage is also not strongly consistent so I don't really have a good solution.

They also employ the "Assume Breach" approach to security, where a lot of work goes into designing security to contain the damage if any given system gets broken into, so you need to break into many things at the same time and go undetected in order to do large amounts of damage

Government and large companies have generally adopted this mindset because it's already the case that a sufficiently resources attacker can find ways to get in to at least a few of your system, so you need to be protected against "escalation of privilege" attacks that are the standard playbooks for leveraging a small breach into a large one. There are several "advanced persistent threats" (mostly believed to be working for the intelligence agencies of a number of major governments) that regularly try to hack into all sorts pf things and very often succeed in getting partial access.

I assume you mean neural cybernetic implants that interface with biological neurons. There are limits to what a neural cybernetic implant can accomplish. The brain is close to thermodynamically optimal at computation under the constraints of operating with 20 Watts while maintaining 37 degree C homeostasis*. So a neural implant can't significantly boost sheer computational power.

But there could be other avenues for improvement - algorithmic efficiency, persistent digital memory, faster rewiring, perfectly copying information, higher bandwidth to the outside world.

But energy efficiency of digital components needs to improve dramatically, otherwise you'll fry your brain.

*https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/xwBuoE9p8GE7RAuhd/brain-efficiency-much-more-than-you-wanted-to-know

I've been wanting for some time to write what I call a Right-Progressive Manifesto, because I despise the way that leftists have been able to capture the word "progressive" and force right-wingers into conservative or reactionary positions. I want to lay out a general direction for what a progressive right would look like, a right with a vision for what real societal progress should look like in a direction that is very much not the direction that the left wants to take us in.

Unfortunately, every time I try to write such a manifesto in my head it gets too long, as I feel obliged to put in a lot of clarifications and preemptions of obvious objections. Instead let me write a sketch of an outline for a manifesto, and perhaps we can take it from there. Here goes:

1. We hold this truth to be self-evident: that all men are created very far from equal, they vary wildly in quality. The best people make society a lot better, the worst people make it a lot worse, and even for the bulk in the middle we find that a lot of them are either net-positive or net-negative.

2. The pleasantness of a society, and of the lives of the people in it, is very largely determined by the quality of the people you share it with. To make society better, we must raise the quality of the people who live in it -- we must gentrify people, not just places.

3. Quality is loosely defined. High quality people make life better for those around them, low quality people make it worse. High quality people are economically productive, creative, non-criminal and do not abuse or drain public resources; low-quality people are the opposite. Quality is about how you actually behave, it is not an intrinsic attribute.

4. A person's quality is determined by several factors: genetics, upbringing/culture, and the decisions they make on a day-to-day basis, which are informed by the incentives which society provides. There is for now not much we can ethically do about genetics, except with immigration policy, so we should focus primarily on the other things.

5. The big questions in society thus become: how can we create the best possible incentives gradient to encourage people to be the best people they can possibly be? How can we change the culture in which people are brought up to encourage people to be the best they can possibly be? Solving these two questions is the key to everything. Unfortunately, the desire to do this is constantly counteracted by leftism, which is why this needs to be a right-progressive manifesto.

Anyway this is already too long despite my best efforts, but you can see the general shape of what I'm getting at.

> My thesis is that "right vs left" maps to a perennial attractor basin that can be recognized throughout history.

I think the actual attractor is "when humans fight against each other, the optimal number of coalitions is two". If you are a small group, your only chances are to join a larger group, so at the end you will get two large groups. But that's about strategy of coalition fighting, not about whether human political opinions can actually be sorted meaningfully into two baskets.

It makes sense for two similar perspectives to end up in the same basket, but many allies are just "at one specific moment it was convenient, so we have invented a rationalization, and since then it is a tradition". A different country could have a different tradition, and a different rationalization why X "naturally is" left or right wing.

Specific example: In USA, gay rights were historically introduced by the left, and the rationalization is "the left cares about the oppressed minorities, and gays are an oppressed minority". But in Slovakia, gay rights were historically introduced by a right-wing party, and the rationalization was "the right cares about freedom, and gays want to be free to do their gay things".

This is more general: the right wing is generally "pro-tradition, pro-history", but the post-communist countries have *two* traditions: the pre-communist history, and the communist history. So they have two flavors of right wing, depending on which moment of the history was their mythical golden age. Similarly, when the communism fell, changing the society towards free market was progressive, in the psychological sense of "hey, let's try new things, it will be awesome".

Sometimes I feel like the natural number of political wings is *three*: (1) aristocratic/theocratic pro-elite conservatism; (2) revolutionary pro-mob woke socialism; and (3) pro-individual pro-chaos libertarianism. And it seems to me that some parts of history could be nicely explained in terms of two weaker groups temporarily joining against the stronger one. When the feudal/religious power is strong, the socialists and the libertarians are united at opposing the monarchy (e.g. the beginning of the French Revolution). When the socialists are strong, the conservatives and libertarians are united at opposing the communist regime. When the libertarians are strong, the conservatives and socialists unite. (The two who unite often take the banner of "freedom", interpreted as "freedom from being ruled by the third one". When they win, it always turns out that by freedom they actually meant the freedom to impose their own rules on the population.)

Yeah, I was kinda sloppy there. I think it's important to distinguish between "attractors" qua "reflective-equilibriums in concept-space" vs "attractors" qua "strategic coalitions in meat-space". I think that the "left vs right" axis used to be the former, but got reinterpreted as a strategic coalition during the Cold War period. Conceptual attractors form axes in an n-dimensional political space, and there's a number of different axes (and the axes themselves represent a compression of information). Then people put themselves somewhere in that space, and form coalitions in a very ad hoc way with whoever's closest.

The idea of Gay Rights being right-coded in Slovakia doesn't surprise me much, since it's not a huge stretch for me to imagine it being pushed by the Libertarian faction of a ColdWar Rightwing coalition. It's all vibes, since coalitions are held together with vibes.

I also suspect that, at the current moment, we're seeing a collapse of the economic narrative everyone inherited from the Cold War. > The big questions in society thus become: how can we create the best possible incentives gradient to encourage people to be the best people they can possibly be? How can we change the culture in which people are brought up to encourage people to be the best they can possibly be? Solving these two questions is the key to everything.

I think the big problem on these is that cultures of excellence / honor / virtus / arete only work at a relatively small and homogenous group size and composition.

So sure, some greek city states had a culture of arete - but it was really only the tiny slice of elite families at the top. Similarly for Rome, there was a couple of hundred nobile families at the top, and they had a culture of virtus within those elites. But it can't actually be a city wide thing, because "character" has to be a common knowledge public virtue, and that breaks down above the greater Dunbar number of ~1500 (Dunbar is most known for his 150 number, but he actually had a whole schema of them, with 1500 the highest tribe-level number and representing "number of people whose face [and presumably reputation] you can recognize").

Moreover, it's probably only a thing with elites due to raw capability, too. Even today, it's only the top quintile or so in America who are NOT grossly overweight, who don't eat junk or fast food for most calories, who have college degrees and higher paying jobs, and so on. What if 80% of people just don't have the conscientiousness or foresight or time preference to actually hold to a high enough standard? That sure looks pretty likely to me.

So that's the real problem then, we basically top out at "a neighborhood" for it to actually work, because an actual village of 1500 is economically irrelevant in the world of today, and high achieving people want restaurants and good grocery stores.

Now, do I think there's something to the idea of some-hundreds-to-low-thousand of people coordinating to try to create that neighborhood together, one where arete is communally upheld as the best way of being? I think it's a great idea!

But it's genuinely hard to coordinate these things. I've tried to get my family all together in the same neighborhood before, even offering to buy the land or land + houses myself. My family is more flexible than most, with entrepreneurship and flexible jobs meaning they're less tied to geography. Still could never get everyone to agree on the same general area, even with me paying for it. I tried for a year at a time twice, looked at several clusters of properties together, and eventually just gave it up. And that's with family! Way easier than trying to coordinate 500 strangers!

Maybe the best regular people can do is something like Alpha schools for their kids, and making your social circle all the other Alpha school parents or something. Or the same idea, but with a specific high achieving church branch.

Still, that seems...less than satisfying or optimal.

So the other solution is higher barriers and more selectivity. People need to be able to self sort on multiple aspects, not just financial, so they can inculcate a culture of excellence at least within their local circle.

Sure, you can buy a fancy house in the best school district, and that's one way, but the cultural alignment isn't all that great - people have money for all sorts of reasons, many unconnected or even anti-correlated with overall character. And with current social mores, the rich are more atomized and less social generally, so you won't get anywhere near the effect you'd get from most families around you valuing the same cultural and character lodestones and being social about it.

Obviously the best outcome would be a whole city that's been sorted like this. Prospera-but-for-arete-nerds. I don't think we'll ever get anything like that, sadly. You'd need some sort of network-state-like clades or phyles or hives social technology for it to happen, and I don't see federal governments giving up autonomy or citizens / tax revenue enough to get to that point anytime soon.

Probably the best you can do for now is moving to Singapore or Tokyo rather than NYC or SF, or some international arbitrage opportunity like that. I've personally done that, and it's very nice to live in a spotless city with excellent public transport, education, and zero homeless people or disorder, but it's an unrealistically huge lift for most people.

I'll hit two quick points that Erica Rall didn't - specifically, that we broke the "we're running out of data" barrier by coming up with techniques that create good enough synthetic data, and related to that, that improvements on the overall manifold of intelligence are surprisingly generalizable, in a very nearly "free lunch" way.

What they ended up doing was training the LLM's on domains with lots of complexity, and known right answers - mathematics, physics, computer programming. Then they took the reasoning traces between the statement of the problem and the answer, and were able to turn that latent high dimensional space of reasoning into an intelligence manifold that could both generalize to other domains, and be used to generate any future data as well.

Interestingly, the reason AI's got better at everything was that the intelligence getting smarter at math and programming unlocks was (perhaps surprisingly) basically generalizable. It was a true free lunch.

There are similar benefits to putting a smarter mind behind vision and robotics goals, and coupled with general architectural tweaks like chunking and iterative generative loops, it solved the "error cumulates over steps" problem around 2023 and led to the big step change in robotics improvement.

There's a pretty interesting video on that where Max Simchowitz talks through why it was such a breakthrough I linked in my last links post, it's here:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UX1YXcRnFbs

Okay, so there's not much out there, because this is so new it's largely an observed empirical thing, and there's actually a lot of competing hypothesis on WHY getting better at hard intelligence generalizes to better soft intelligence.

So I should have been a little more clear about the process, I was just sort of whipping that first comment off. But basically the big factors are 1) scaling - the reason there's papers titled "scaling is all you need" is because that's generally true - if you can throw more compute and data at a given problem, the transformer architecture lets you get smarter and better, although to maintain the same scaling requires 10x higher investments each time. And this is why we're spending half a trillion dollars on more data centers.

So 2) is the whole intelligence manifold thing - specifically if you do RL on the reasoning traces to the final known trues (this is RLVR), you build general reasoning capacities that can generalize to narratives, personal advice, and softer intelligence.

Here's an interesting and pretty readable paper on that.

https://arxiv.org/html/2507.00432v2

3) WHY it generalizes is still an open question.

The Deepseek guys say that unrestricted RL reasoning (ie no humans in the loop) actually leads to novel self-learned reasoning strategies that would otherwise not be found, ie this opens up a wider reasoning space and the models get some lift from that.

https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-025-09422-z

Lots of papers show that including code in pretraining greatly enhances final model capability. Code enforces logical dependencies, variable tracking, conditional branching, and compositional reasoning. Training on code may teach the model to track state and compose operations in ways that transfer to natural language reasoning. This is the most commonly cited hypothesis but remains largely empirical rather than mechanistically proven.

Some people argue that RLVR is actually just making a change in the overall probability allocation for the smarter reasoning traces, and that those traces always existed in the base model, it would have just taken a lot longer to find them without the RLVR. This is sort of gesturing at a reason why RLVR might be better - and this is contra to the Deepseek's team idea.

So it's currently just an observed empirical fact that people are trying to decode and decompose into elements to understand why it's generalizing, and is an active area of inquiry right now.

> Training on code may teach the model to track state and compose operations in ways that transfer to natural language reasoning.

> Some people argue that RLVR is actually just making a change in the overall probability allocation for the smarter reasoning traces, and that those traces always existed in the base model, it would have just taken a lot longer to find them without the RLVR.

Naively, I'd expect that both would be true. "hard" disciplines like math and programming are just a subspace of reasoning where invariants of the territory become ironclad guarantees (AKA constraints) in the map. Soft-reasoning is like a Kalman filter, where error more-easily compounds since the trajectory of reasoning is subject to drift.

This relates to the common critique of Bay Area Bayesianism: as soon as you put a number on a belief (without contextualizing it in a DAG), you're collapsing a high-dimensional but fuzzy model of reality into 1-dimensional yet maximally-precise numerical-figure.

>Do they use the same technology so it's obvious that when one advances, the other advances?

It's mostly this one, at least for image generation and LLMs. Both areas are built around a core of throwing enormous amounts of compute and practically the entire internet into training a conceptually relatively simple neural network.

The key bits I'm aware of are:

1. Google invested heavily in Neural Network research in the 2010s, had tons of data to train them on, and had a good system for using spare capacity in existing datacenters. This produced proofs of concept of the basic approaches underlying LLMs and image generation (Word2Vec and DeepDream, respectively), both of which were published and available for others to build upon.

2. Both use GPUs for training. The techniques and hardware for doing distributed neural network training on GPUs on the necessary scale came together relatively recently.

3. OpenAI demonstrated that LLMs were marketable products, which opened the floodgates for everyone else who had been doing research on either LLMs or image generation to spend "major product initiative" amounts of money on them instead of "speculative long-term R&D" amounts of money.

I tried posing this question to DeepSeek R1 in think mode. The thought dialogue is largely about how to come up with an answer that meets the requirement of being surprising. It “should be something that makes people pause and reconsider their assumptions about how the world works.” Its planned answer to the question is to make sleep optional. This answer identifies a “specific, tangible change.” The plan is to explain that this change creates a “surprising cascade of effects, especially the psychological one: realizing that most of our stress about "not enough hours" was artificially created by a biological quirk.”

Deepseek has gone off the rails here; in practice if sleep were optional then expectations would change and the number of people stressing out about "not enough hours" would probably be the same.

The actual answer begins: “That's a fascinating question, because the "surprising" angle forces me to move beyond the usual fixes like ending poverty or curing disease.

“So here's my surprising change: I would make it impossible for any human to feel embarrassed or ashamed about asking for help.”

I think this is a good answer. Deepseek goes on to say that this change “sounds small” but would bring about widespread improvements in a variety of areas.

So Deepseek is essentially trying to come up with an idea for improving the world that appears in its training data and that meets three criteria for being surprising: (1) it would not be a common answer to a similar question that did not request a surprising answer, (2) it is fairly specific/concrete, and (3) the ways it would improve the world are not immediately obvious.

I think that some variant of “change human nature” is the answer to this question because lots of people complain about human nature but most discourse treats human nature as immutable.

Honestly, I'd probably do something about the baseline texture of ordinary human consciousness — not dramatic suffering, but the ambient, normalized unhappiness that's so common it's largely invisible. The low-grade anxiety, the sense of inadequacy, the way most people experience their own minds as somewhat hostile environments most of the time.

That's surprising for a few reasons:

It's not on the standard list. Climate, war, poverty, disease — those are the obvious targets. But I think a lot of what makes those problems so hard to solve is that humans are operating from a baseline state that makes clear thinking, long-term cooperation, and genuine empathy harder than they need to be.

It's also not obviously an external problem, which makes it feel illegitimate to name. We treat suffering caused by circumstance as real and worth fixing, but treat suffering that's just... how minds work... as either inevitable or a personal failing.

And it sidesteps the power concentration problem I raised earlier in a way I find interesting — rather than reshaping institutions or resources, it's changing the substrate from which humans make their own choices. Though I'll immediately admit that's also a deeply paternalistic intervention, possibly more so than any other option. Changing how people feel from the inside without their consent is arguably the most invasive thing imaginable.

So the surprising answer contains its own serious problem, which might be why it doesn't get proposed much.

The 3rd amendment to the U.S. Constitution states: "No Soldier shall, in time of peace be quartered in any house, without the consent of the Owner, nor in time of war, but in a manner to be prescribed by law." It's commonly joked that the so-called "framers" were being laughably naive in assuming this would continue to be relevant after the Revolutionary War ended, and for the most part that has rung true, but perhaps not anymore?

I only found out about this from a random youtube recommendation but: an investigative journalist thinks there may be a legitimate 3rd amendment challenge regarding Oracle's integration of Medicare and Medicaid data into the same interoperable database infrastructure that they're integrating with Palantir to host Top Secret Special Access Programs (SAPs) or other Top Secret programs requiring SCI clearance, the highest level publicly acknowledged. What this means, according to the argument laid out in the article below (and its accompanying video), is that people working for the U.S. government in a military capacity would have access to private health records for anyone using Medicare or Medicaid, i.e. millions of people.

Now you may still be thinking "what does this have to do with the 3rd amendment?", which is fair. But there is an argument based on the only federal case in which the 3rd amendment was ever tested, Laird v Tatum (1972), that the words "house" and "owner" are used metaphorically in such a way that Oracle's "data lakehouse" would count, since Oracle's own documentation uses words like "tenant" to refer to the person who is "renting" the storage space, which could conceivably apply to anyone whose data is stored on Oracle's infrastructure.

I promise this is not self promotion; I did not write this article and have no connection with the person who did. I just thought it was interesting.

https://thedreydossier.substack.com/p/its-time-drey-v-oracle

Counterpoint: our language and concepts are fundamentally built on metaphor. The first amendment protects freedom of "speech" and of the "press", but at the time it was written, people understood that this didn't just mean a literal printing press or literal vocalizations. They also intended to protect handwritten works, I'm sure they meant laser printers to be included if they knew what those were, in fact they intended it to be pretty general. I think there's a pretty solid case that it protects websites, for example, even though there may be no pressing or vocalizing involved. So you can't escape this sort of metaphorical generalization with the constitution, because it's definitely in there.

I do find the Oracle case pretty doubtful, though. While "quartering in a house" could be taken metaphorically in some respects (does it count if someone lives on a boat? If a human can study either of those things, then a human can plausibly study both of them (even if they won't reach the top expertise in either). Such humans can be translators from X to Y.

The problem is that for N sciences, we will need O(N^2) translators.

We could go even further and train people to be specifically translators and nothing else, so they could handle even four or five different sciences. I mean, if we only required them to have good understanding of each, not to produce research in each.

We could add another layer, and make specialized teachers of each science, who specialize at teaching the translators. Try to teach as much as is necessary to understand the science at a certain level, as easily as possible, but not a little more because the time is precious and your student is expected to learn a few other sciences, too.

A major headwind in the face of our current system of electoral democracy is simply the rising complexity of the world which politics have to navigate and tame.

Compare the number of domains out of reach of a functional understanding of the median voter in 1900 and today:

Back then? Banking/insurance/financing and perhaps some finer points of law and international trade. Everything else about society and how it operates was very grokable.

Today? Agriculture, manufacturing, energy production, logistics, supply chains, medicine, electronics, satellites, surveillance, the internet, international relations & institutions and many other indispensable phenomena are completely opaque black boxes to most people. And yet, they are expected to wisely and responsibly elect representatives who will deal with them on societal and global level.

The complexity of the metaphorical political conveyance in which our civilization is riding has grown exponentially. The average person may be reasonably expected to broadly know how a bus works; They can't realistically be asked to understand a stealth bomber.

EngineOfCreation

The degree to which the average person understands a thing to at least a basic level has more to do with familiarity than some inherent complexity of the thing. Humans are fundamentally the same, and if one person can train a lot to understand a topic deeply, then another person can train (formally or not) to understand it superficially.

For example, the average Ukrainian understands modern drone warfare much better than the average non-Ukrainian. Does that mean the average Ukrainian is somehow blessed with a supernatural understanding of drone warfare? Of course not; the reason is that it's their daily, lived reality. They can then make electoral decisions based on that knowledge, should it be relevant. Everyone else's picture is limited by disinterest and remoteness, and the gaps are being filled in by fiction, propaganda, media in general. But they wouldn't vote in Ukraine, so what does it matter as far democracy is concerned?

Same for your pre-industrial village farmboy and his general - warfare was basically a fact of life for most people. It didn't change much over a human lifespan, sure, but nobody is born with that knowledge, and humans are generally quite capable of adapting to new knowledge from new situations - as long as it's immediately relevant to their lives.

At the same time, how much does it all matter? You run the risk of proposing an arbitrary line of understanding and declaring it problematic when some arbitrary percentage of people can't clear that line. One basic understanding of warfare is that "people kill each other in somewhat organized fashion over something they want.", which really hasn't changed at all and which your farmboy would readily understand if he was magically teleported into today. Whether the details look like the Napoleonic Wars, WW1, WW2, Vietnam, Afghanistan, or Ukraine can be relevant, but doesn't have to be. It always depends on the question that needs answering, and as I argued in my first comment, democracy as a concept does not depend on it.
